coredata.dc:description: Developing a novel fluorescence carbon-based sensor with high sensitivity, selectivity, and rapid response is critical for detecting ferric ion (Fe3 +) in beverages and food products. Overcoming this challenge is essential, as excess Fe3 + can cause cellular damage, raising health concerns. Herein, we designed a fluorescence nanosensor utilizing a hybrid iron oxide/carbon dots (HICs) nanocomposite, synthesized from blue pea (BPs) flowers and iron oxide nanoparticles through a hydrothermal treatment. More importantly, the HICs demonstrated high sensitivity and a rapid response to Fe3 +, with a linear detection range spanning 0.06 to 100 µM and an impressively low limit of detection (LOD) of 13.61 nM. Moreover, the HICs exhibited remarkable selectivity toward Fe3 +, remaining unaffected by various interferences, including common metal ions and small organic molecules. Upon Fe3 + addition, the fluorescence quenching of HICs occurs through a combination of static quenching and dynamic quenching mechanism and the inner filter effect. Additionally, the detection of Fe3 + in milk and herbal drinks was achieved using HICs, yielding satisfactory recovery rates ranging from 94.4 % to 103.7 %. Thus, the developed HICs serve as highly efficient “turn-off’” fluorescent nanosensors, exhibiting a rapid response, high sensitivity, and ultra-selectivity for Fe3 + detection, unaffected by matrix interference. These qualities make them promising candidates for advanced detection applications in food products, ensuring food safety.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing a novel fluorescence carbon-based sensor with high sensitivity, selectivity, and rapid response is critical for detecting ferric ion (Fe3 +) in beverages and food products. Overcoming this challenge is essential, as excess Fe3 + can cause cellular damage, raising health concerns. Herein, we designed a fluorescence nanosensor utilizing a hybrid iron oxide/carbon dots (HICs) nanocomposite, synthesized from blue pea (BPs) flowers and iron oxide nanoparticles through a hydrothermal treatment. More importantly, the HICs demonstrated high sensitivity and a rapid response to Fe3 +, with a linear detection range spanning 0.06 to 100 µM and an impressively low limit of detection (LOD) of 13.61 nM. Moreover, the HICs exhibited remarkable selectivity toward Fe3 +, remaining unaffected by various interferences, including common metal ions and small organic molecules. Upon Fe3 + addition, the fluorescence quenching of HICs occurs through a combination of static quenching and dynamic quenching mechanism and the inner filter effect. Additionally, the detection of Fe3 + in milk and herbal drinks was achieved using HICs, yielding satisfactory recovery rates ranging from 94.4 % to 103.7 %. Thus, the developed HICs serve as highly efficient “turn-off’” fluorescent nanosensors, exhibiting a rapid response, high sensitivity, and ultra-selectivity for Fe3 + detection, unaffected by matrix interference. These qualities make them promising candidates for advanced detection applications in food products, ensuring food safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ferric ion (Fe3+) is a biologically essential mineral for all living entities, found in foods, dairy products, dietary supplements, and the environment. It plays a crucial role in numerous physiological processes, including hemoglobin formation, oxygen transport through the bloodstream, enzyme activity, DNA and RNA replication and repair, metabolism, and electron transfer within cellular biological processes. Particularly within the pediatric population, it is integral to the synthesis of certain hormones, physical growth, and neurological development [1–4]. Apart from their importance in physiological processes, these indicators also serve as warning signs for a range of highly concerning diseases, including anemia, cardiovascular diseases, heart disease, Parkinson's disease, neurological disorders, cancers, and hemochromatosis— a genetic disorder [1,3,5]. All of these can be attributed to a deficiency or excessive intake of Fe3+, leading to an imbalance in intercellular iron levels. Cases of iron deficiency anemia in infancy can lead to irreversible cognitive and psychological effects, such as delayed attention and social isolation. Nonetheless, to avoid the risk of insufficient Fe3+ intake and ensure health and food safety, it is essential to adhere to guidelines regarding daily Fe3+ consumption. The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) establishes the Recommended Dietary Allowance (RDA) for average daily iron intake at 18 mg per day for individuals over 4 years old, including both children and adults, with adjustments made for specific age and sex requirements. Beyond that, food safety must be considered in diets. The European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) reported that the Tolerable Upper Intake Level (UL) of Fe3+ was set at 5, 10, and 40 mg per day for infants, minors, and adults, including pregnant and lactating women. If the high-dose intake of Fe3+ exceeds limits, there is a risk of adverse effects, such as gastrointestinal distress, nausea, and constipation. It has been reported that ingesting iron doses exceeding 40 mg per kg of body weight is extremely lethal due to hemorrhagic necrosis [5]. Thus, the detection of Fe3+ in diets, beverages, dairy products, and herbal drinks is a great aspect of assessing diet quality and diet guidance. A slight variation in its amount can significantly impact the activity of human health. Nevertheless, the accurate detection of Fe3+ at low concentrations is extremely challenging in the presence of other interfering ions in real samples. So far, several techniques have been developed for Fe3+ detection; however, their practical applications are limited by the complexity of the procedures, high costs, and the need for extensive data analysis. Currently, fluorescence nanosensors are widely required due to their unique advantages, including ease of operation, cost-effectiveness, rapid response, high sensitivity, and accurate detection of hazardous metal cations at low concentrations in natural environments, food items, and body fluids [6].

Carbon dots (CDs) represent advanced carbon-based nanomaterials extensively explored for their utility as fluorescence nanosensors in Fe3+ detection. They are synthesized from biomass and small organic molecules, serving as precursors for functionalizing the hydrophilic surface with carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. Additionally, heteroatom doping is employed to enhance the optical properties of CDs. Notably, CDs exhibit superior fluorescence characteristics, exceptional photostability, high water solubility, minimal toxicity, and biocompatibility [7,8]. Consequently, CDs offer substantial promise for diverse applications, particularly in chemical and metal ion sensing, bioimaging, cancer therapeutics, disease diagnosis, and photocatalysis [4,9,10]. Extensive research has recently validated the efficacy of CDs in detecting Fe3+. These include nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs), which have gained significant attention for detecting metal ions and organic molecules, thanks to their enhanced fluorescence and improved detection sensitivity and selectivity. Cui, J. et al. developed NCDs from DL-malic acid and urea, serving as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively, and employed them as a “turn-off” sensor for Fe3+ detection, achieving a remarkable limit of detection (LOD) of 1.9 μM. The interaction of Fe3+ ions resulted in fluorescence quenching through a static quenching mechanism (SQM) coupled with an inner filter effect (IFE) [11].Chen, Y. et al. synthesized NCDs using L-glutamic acid and ethylenediamine through a microwave-assisted process, achieving a sensitive turn-off response for Fe3+ detection with a LOD of 3.8 µM, and these NCDs can be applied for intracellular Fe3+ imaging in fungal cells [12]. Beyond this, several studies have focused on designing surface functional groups of CDs from natural plants. For example, Ge, G. et al. developed a bright blue fluorescence probe for selective Fe3+ sensing and cellular imaging, with CDs synthesized from fresh tea leaves (Camellia sinensis (L.)) and urea via a one-step hydrothermal method. This probe demonstrated a LOD of 0.079 µM, along with low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, and long-term stability [13]. Subsequently, Atchudan, R. et al. employed leftover kiwi fruit peel (Actinidia deliciosa) and ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH) to produce NCDs via a hydrothermal carbonization process. These NCDs were utilized as fluorescent probes, achieving quenching of Fe3+ with a LOD of 0.85 µM [14].

Meanwhile, magnetite nanoparticles (Fe3O4 NPs), a type of iron oxide, offer unique properties, such as biocompatibility, low toxicity, high-temperature stability, and superparamagnetic behavior, making them ideal for a variety of biological applications, including biosensors, drug delivery, biological imaging, bio-separation, and cancer therapy [15–17]. Despite their merits, Fe3O4 NPs have limitations, including a tendency to agglomerate due to hydrophobic properties and oxidize when exposed to air, causing a decline in magnetic and colloidal strength. Thus, enhancing the stability of Fe3O4 NPs requires the application of a protective shell. They must be coated with suitable materials to protect against oxidation, like silica (SiO2) [17], ethylene glycol (EG) [18], polyethylene glycol (PEG) [19], polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) [20], and chitosan [21].

Recent strides in nanotechnology have driven the advancement of hybrid iron oxide/carbon dot (HICs) as novel fluorescence nanosensors, offering solutions to previous challenges and gaining crucial traction in biosensing applications, including lysine [16] and tetracycline (TCy) [22,23]. Remarkably, HICs are driving the development of nanohybrid materials that integrate Fe3O4 NPs and CDs to enhance optical properties and selectively target molecules for biosensing, thereby vastly expanding their applications. Sudewi, S. et al. designed HICs from Fe3O4 NPs and citric acid (CA) for the “turn on” mode of lysine with a LOD of 0.66 μM [16]. Furthermore, they reported a fluorescence probe of HICs for “turn off” mode of TCy detection in urine, using Fe3O4 NPs with glutamic acid [22] and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) [23]. Despite the extensive applications of fluorescent sensors, there are still concerns regarding environmental and diet safety hazards. Consequently, there remains an ongoing demand for the development of convenient, effective, and eco-friendly fluorescent sensors in diets and beverages [24]. Additionally, the cytotoxicity of HICs has been studied using iron (III) acetylacetonate with ethanolamine and citric acid as precursors. As a result, HICs indicate low toxicity, as HeLa cell viability remains above 85 % (200 μg/mL) for various HICs. Furthermore, HICs demonstrate fluorescent bioimaging capabilities in HeLa cells. These findings suggest that HICs are promising safe and environment-friendly fluorescent nanosensors [25]. There is only one report on the use of HICs for Fe3+ adsorption and removal; however, the sensing materials were based on L-cysteine-capped Fe3O4/ZnO [26], which was complicated and rather expensive. Therefore, the development of low-cost, high-sensitivity sensing materials for Fe3+ ions is highly desired.

As mentioned above, triggering an interest in improving the fluorescence properties of HICs for specific and quantitative detection of Fe3+ at low concentrations remains challenging. The vital role of HICs lies in surface functional group engineering, including surface passivation and heteroatom doping, which are often employed. These strategies are critical for enhancing the effectiveness of HICs in sensing applications and improving their stability, selectivity, and sensitivity for Fe3+ detection. Hence, in this work, we designed blue emissive HICs via a solvothermal treatment, using Fe3O4 NPs as the iron oxide source and blue pea flowers (BPs) as the nitrogen, oxygen, and carbon sources of CDs.

Among various natural plant sources, blue pea (Clitoria ternatea L.) flowers were selected as promising new natural precursors for creating CDs nanomaterials due to their rich content of polyphenols [27], flavonoids [28], anthocyanins [28], alkaloids [29], cyclotides [30], histidine [31], and threonine [31]. These components serve as the sources of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen, enabling the doping of the surface with nitrogen and oxygen functional groups. The BPs also possess the benefit of therapeutic properties, such as anti-inflammatory, anti-oxidant, anti-Alzheimer’s, anti-cancer, and effects, along with low toxicity and biocompatibility [31,32], making it an attractive choice. Notably, Tak, K. et al. synthesized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from BPs via a one-pot microwave-assisted method for Alzheimer’s disease treatment [31]. Additionally, Barus, D. A., et al. used BPs and urea to synthesize NCDs as environmentally friendly nanosensors for detecting Cu2+ ions, achieving a LOD of 183.33 nM [32]. Although the BPs have been used as precursors for synthesizing CDs, most of their applications have focused on other properties. However, no reports have yet been published on the fabrication of nanohybrid materials of HICs using Fe3O4 NPs and BPs as a candidate for ultra-selective fluorescence nanosensors for Fe3+ detection.

The prepared HICs exhibited highly sensitive and selective performance in determining Fe3+, with a demonstrated linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and Fe3+ concentration ranging from 0.06 to 100 μM, and a LOD of 13.6 nM. Also, we investigated the fluorescence-quenching mechanism of HICs with Fe3+, which was due to the SQM and IFE, given the large overlap in the peak areas between the fluorescence spectra of HICs and the ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum of Fe3+. These results suggest the “turn-off” fluorescent nanosensor, with its rapid response, high effectiveness, and sensitivity for Fe3+ detection. Therefore, HICs are environmentally safe nanosensors with the ability to accurately detect Fe3+ in biological systems and foods, including processed foods, dairy products, and herbal drinks.

All chemicals used were of at least analytical grade or above. Iron (III) chloride (FeCl3), sodium acetate anhydrous, and ethanol were bought from Xilong Chemicals (China). Ethylene glycol (EG), tri-sodium citrate 2-hydrate were purchased from Kemaus. Polyethylene glycol 400 (PEG-400), phosphate-buffered saline (10X PBS), metal ions including, Bi2O3, CdCl3, AlCl3 FeCl2, PbCl2, CoCl2, NiCl2, NaCl, LiCl, CuCl2, and small organic compounds, such as ammonia, urea, thiourea, caffeine, L-alanine, L-cysteine, glycine, arbutin, L-ascorbic acid, D-gluconic acid, citric acid, tartaric acid, mandelic acid, and D-glucose were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Real samples of ferric ions in milk and herbal drinks were purchased from a supermarket in Kaohsiung, Taiwan, while BPs were sourced from a supermarket in Thailand. Stock solutions of Fe3+ ions and other analytes were prepared in deionized (DI) water (resistivity > 18 MΩ·cm).

Fluorescence spectra and decay lifetime of HICs were measured using a Horiba Fluoromax-4 spectrometer, while UV–vis spectra were obtained with a Shimadzu UV-1900 spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscopy energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (TEM-EDX) characterization was conducted on a JEM-2100Plus with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The attenuated total reflection Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectrum was recorded using a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS50 FT-IR spectrometer, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed with a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250 Microprobe. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were acquired using a Rigaku X-ray diffractometer with an angular resolution of 0.010° over a range of 10–80° at a speed of 3°/min.

FeCl3 (4 mmol) and the electrostatic stabilizer, trisodium citrate (0.68 mmol), were dissolved in 50 mL of EG. This mixture was then combined with a sodium acetate alkali source (1.2 mg) and stirred for 30 min. The solution was subsequently heated in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 200 °C for 10 h using a solvothermal treatment [22]. After fabrication, Fe3O4 NPs with a black appearance were obtained, purified by magnetic separation, and rinsed three times with deionized water and ethanol.

The Fe3O4 NPs (90 mg) and PEG-400 (200 µg) were dissolved in 50 mL of EG, followed by 30 min of sonication. Subsequently, the blue pea flower was introduced into the solution in a varying ratio of 0.5, 0.6, 0.8, and 1.0 g, resulting in a series of HICs designated as HICs-1, HICs-2, HICs-3, and HICs-4, respectively. Following this, the solutions underwent sonication for 30 min, were stirred for 1 h, and were heated in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 200 °C for 14 h via solvothermal treatment. Eventually, the dark brown liquid of HICs was obtained after cooling to room temperature, which was purified by filtration through a filtration membrane and stored at 4 °C for future use. Additionally, CDs were synthesized under the same conditions without the Fe3O4 NPs, and these CDs were used as a reference control to compare properties with the HICs series.

The quantum yield (QY) of the fluorescent HICs solution was assessed using quinine sulfate as a reference (QY = 54 %), which was dispersed in a 0.1 M H2SO4 solution. Utilizing the following Eq.

The subscripts C and R denote the HICs and the reference material, respectively. Φ is the quantum yield, I is the integrated emission intensity, A is the absorbance, and n is the refractive index.

The photostability of HICs was assessed by recording their photoluminescence (PL) spectra under UV light (λ365 nm) at intervals of 5 min for 1 h, following the 360-nm excitation wavelength. The ionic strength effect on HICs solutions was observed at varying concentrations of NaCl (0 to 1000 mM) in aqueous solutions. Additionally, the UV absorption spectra were utilized to determine the photostability of the HICs, which were recorded at intervals of 1 h for 6 h. Furthermore, the long-term stability of HICs was monitored in different pH conditions that were recorded under a refrigerator (4 °C) for 1 month.

To evaluate the selectivity of the HICs-based fluorescence nanosensor for detection of coexisting Fe3+, it was investigated alongside, at first, multiple metal ions (Bi3+, Cd2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Na+, Li+, and Cu2+) and different small organic compounds (L-ascorbic acid, citric acid, tartaric acid, gluconic acid, mandelic acid, D-glucose, ammonia, urea, thiourea, L-alanine, glycine, caffeine, and arbutin) as interference species. The investigation involved observing the relative fluorescence response between HICs (0.022 mg⋅mL−1) and each interference species (200 μM) in a pH 4 solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample analysis and detecting of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the sensitivity of HICs used as nanosensors, the LOD for Fe3+ was systematically investigated across a concentration range from 0.06 to 500 μM. Following a 5 min mixing period, the PL intensity of the HICs was measured to explore the correlation between the reduction in PL intensity and the varying concentrations of Fe3+. The practical applicability of the HICs sensor for Fe3+ detection was further validated through real sample analysis, including milk and herbal drinks, to confirm their effectiveness in this sensing method. The herbal drink sample was filtered through the 0.22 μm nylon membrane.

Whilst the milk sample was incubated at 40 °C, anhydrous Na2SO4 (20 g/100 mL) was added, and the mixture was stirred continuously to ensure complete solubility. Following these processes, the precipitate became apparent and was subsequently removed using Whatman No. 4 filter paper. To ensure the complete elimination of any remaining proteins and fats, anhydrous Na2SO4 was introduced, followed by H2SO4 to facilitate the precipitation of residual proteins. Then, it was filtered through a 0.22 μm nylon membrane. Milk and herbal drink samples (5 mL) were diluted with deionized water to 100 mL. Subsequently, 1 mL of the diluted sample solution was combined with 1 mL of the HICs solution and spiked with a stock solution of Fe3+ (3 mM) to gain a final concentration of 20–100 μM. The PL intensity was recorded upon excitation at 320 nm, with each sample measured in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Fe3O4 NPs and HICs
ce:section.ce:para:
In the establishment of biocompatible fluorescence nanosensors, HICs were designed through a hybrid of hydrophilic Fe3O4 NPs and CDs, which can be converted into HICs. In this publication, Deng, H., et al. synthesized hydrophilic Fe3O4 NPs using a solvothermal treatment by reducing FeCl3 with EG. It is well known that EG, a simple representative of polyols, can act as a stabilizer, with excess EG serving as both solvent and reducing agent [33]. However, the resulting Fe3O4 NPs were still not sufficiently water dispersible [34]. Therefore, in the primary step, we designed highly water-dispersible Fe3O4 NPs, as illustrated in Scheme 1 a. The synthesis route involved FeCl3 dispersion in EG in the presence of trisodium citrate (Na3Cit) and sodium acetate as alkali sources via solvothermal treatment. Na3Cit also acted as an electrostatic stabilizer, enhancing the water dispersibility of Fe3O4 NPs by increasing the negative charge density on the surface, as the citrate groups embedded onto the surface due to the strong affinity of the COOH groups in Na3Cit for Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions. This, in turn, prevented particle aggregation and improved stability [35]. Moreover, thermal energy induced the crystallization of Fe3O4 NPs after the reduction of Fe3+ in FeCl3, spontaneously forming oxyhydroxide between iron (III) oxyhydroxide (FeO·OH) and iron(II) hydroxide [Fe(OH)2], which were transformed into homogeneous Fe3O4 according to the reaction in Eq.
(2) [36].
(2) Fe(OH)2 + 2FeO·OH → Fe3O4 + 2H2O

Likewise, PEG has been utilized to enhance the stability of Fe3O4 NPs in aqueous dispersion, addressing concerns about their tendency to agglomerate and oxidize in water. As a non-toxic polyol, PEG is used for surface stabilization of Fe3O4 NPs through its numerous hydroxyl groups, which generate carboxyl groups that adsorb onto the nanoparticle surfaces, improving colloidal stability [37,38]. This modification not only prevents aggregation and oxidation but also enhances the biocompatibility of Fe3O4 NPs, making them more suitable for various biomedical and environmental applications. In the secondary step, the modification of the hydrophilic surface and the aqueous dispersibility of the HICs played a crucial role in synthesizing materials with sensor properties. This was achieved by using Fe3O4 NPs in the presence of EG, coated with PEG-400, and functionalized with oxygen and nitrogen groups from BPs through solvothermal treatment (Scheme 1b). Furthermore, to demonstrate the enhanced optical properties, particularly the fluorescence intensity, a series of HICs were designed with varying BP contents of 0.5, 0.6, 0.8, and 1.0 g, labeled as HICs-1, HICs-2, HICs-3, and HICs-4, respectively.

The morphology and particle size distributions of the Fe3O4 NPs and HICs-4 were thoroughly analyzed and validated via the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) technique, as depicted in Fig. 1 . As illustrated in Fig. 1a, an aggregate of Fe3O4 NPs was observed, and the interplanar spacing was determined to be 0.21 and 0.25 nm, corresponding to the (400) and (311) planes, respectively [39,40]. Nevertheless, HICs-4 exhibited monodispersion without apparent aggregation in EG, displaying a spherical-like morphology attributed to the influence of PEG-400 and hydrophilic functional groups of CDs that ensured well-dispersed particles, as presented in Fig. 1b. This effect is due to PEG effectively stabilizing the oxide materials by promoting the formation of carboxyl-functionalized surfaces on carbon structures. Moreover, the HRTEM image of HICs-4 illustrates a core–shell structure, with darker Fe3O4 and lighter CDs parts. Upon enlarging the picture, the interplanar spacing was determined to be 0.21 and 0.25 nm, corresponding to the (400) and (311) lattice planes of Fe3O4 NPs, respectively. Moreover, the Fe3O4 NPs and HICs-4 exhibited an average size distribution of 7.6 and 20.9 nm, respectively, as illustrated in Fig. 1(c-d). Thus, the CDs shell is about 6.7 nm thick. To evaluate the composition of the Fe3O4 NPs, energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy was employed (Fig. S1a). The EDX spectra reveal the presence of Fe, O, and C in the Fe3O4 NPs, but Cu signals were still observed due to the use of copper TEM grids. The superparamagnetic nature of Fe3O4 NPs was therefore studied using the magnetic separation technique, as shown in Fig. S1b. The digital image showed the dispersion of Fe3O4 NPs in the presence and the absence of an external magnet. The Fe3O4 NPs displayed a transparent solution in the presence of an external magnet, suggesting that the Fe3O4 exhibited paramagnetic behavior. Fig. S2 illustrates that the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the crystalline structure of Fe3O4 NPs exhibited prominent reflection peaks at 18.8°, 30.1°, 35.5°, 43.1°, 53.4°, 62.6°, and 74.3°. These peaks corresponded to the (111), (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), (440), and (553) planes, and they closely matched with Fe3O4 NPs reference found on the Joint Committee on Powder Diffraction Standards (JCPDS, 96–900–7645) [41]. This evidence confirms the presence of a Fe3O4 NPs crystalline cubic spinel structure. Due to the presence of CDs shell surrounding the Fe3O4 core, the HICs-4 diffraction pattern showed a broad peak around 22.5° of the (002) plane, indicative of the appearance of amorphous CDs in graphite structures [11] as shown in Fig. S3. Because of the dominant broad peak of the CDs, only peaks at 35.5° and 62.6° of the (311) and (440) planes of Fe3O4 NPs emerged. Therefore, these results affirm the successful synthesis of the HICs, and HICs consisted of both Fe3O4 and CDs compositions.

The surface structures and chemical compositions of the Fe3O4 NPs and all HICs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses as presented in Fig. S4 (a-f). The FTIR spectra displayed the absorption bands at 1574 and 1393 cm−1, associated with the carboxylate groups of Na3Cit, stabilizing the Fe3O4 NPs [35,36] (Fig. S4a). The characteristic bands of HICs-1 and HICs-4 were identified as the intense absorption peaks at 3357 cm−1, attributing to the broad O − H and N − H stretching vibrations, and at 2876 and 2917 cm−1, corresponding to the symmetric and asymmetric C–H stretching vibrations, respectively [42]. The peak intensities of the oxygenated functional groups in the HICs observed at 1712, 1595, and 1350 cm−1 corresponded to the C=O (COOH) stretching, similar to those in bare Fe3O4 NPs and BPs [16]. Additionally, the peaks at 1440, 1247, 1069, and 949 cm−1 are associated with the C=C aromatic, C-O-C, C-O/C-N stretching, and O–H bending, respectively, matching those found in BPs [8,43,44]. Notably, the intensity of these peaks for oxygenated and nitrogenated functional groups in HICs-4 increased with higher BPs content, from HICs-1 to HICs-4. Lastly, the Fe-O vibrational band of HICs observed at 517 cm−1 decreased significantly [40,45]. The significant decrease in Fe-O peak intensity in HICs indicates that the Fe3O4 NPs were effectively stabilized by hydroxyl groups from PEG, carboxyl groups from Na3Cit, and surrounded by the CDs.

The XPS survey spectra of the HICs and BPs show peaks of C1s (284.7 eV), N1s (400.0 eV), and O1s (532.3 eV), while the Fe2p (710.7 − 724.6 eV) peaks, observed in Fe3O4 NPs, exhibited similar atomic concentrations as presented in Fig. S4b. The high carbon content in Fe3O4 NPs is due to the presence of Na3Cit on the surface of the nanoparticles, whereas the high carbon content in HICs arose from the presence of CDs surrounding the nanoparticles as a shell. The comparison of HICs prepared with varying BP concentrations (from 0.5 to 1.0 g) shows a multitude of oxygen-related concentrations.Similarly, an increase in nitrogen concentration aligns with the amounts of BPs added to the synthesis. It is as expected because BPs contain nitrogen-containing molecules, such as alkaloids, cyclotides (macrocyclic peptides), histidine, and threonine, suggesting that BPs could be a valuable source for further research on nitrogen functional groups in HICs [46]. Herein, the high-resolution spectra of C1s of all samples illustrate the C=C (284.6 eV) and O-C=O (288.5 eV) units of COOH and C-N/C-O (285.9 eV) present in HICs-1 and HICs-4 (Fig. S4c) [11,47]. The N1s spectra of HICs-1 and HICs-4 revealed a pyridinic C-N-C structure, similar to that in BPs (Fig. S4d). Additionally, the O1s signal of all samples showed peaks at O=C-O (532.2 eV) and C=O (531.9 eV) in BPs and Fe3O4, with Fe-O (529.5 eV) observed only in Fe3O4 NPs [2,48] The absence of Fe-O signal in HICs is due to the limited penetration depth of X-ray to reach the Fe3O4 core with a 5–10 nm CDs shell (Fig. S4e). Besides, the Fe2p signal was deconvoluted into two peaks at 710.3 eV and 723.9 eV, attributed to Fe (III)2p3/2 and Fe (II)2p1/2, respectively [47], which belonged to the Fe3O4 NPs (Fig. S4f). The Fe peaks of HICs-4 were attenuated due to the presence of CDs surrounding the surface of Fe3O4 NPs as a core. This is consistent with the disappearance of Fe-O in Fig. S4e. Although the Fe2p peaks were not observed, the successful encapsulation of CDs on Fe3O4 was confirmed by an increase in size, carbon content, XRD, and FT-IR results as described above. Therefore, both FT-IR and XPS analyses confirmed the successful surface functionalization of the HICs with amine, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups through the incorporation of C, N, and O atoms.

To investigate the optical properties of the HICs series solutions, the UV − vis absorption spectra and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were collected and presented in Fig. S5(a-b). Fig. S5a demonstrates that the HICs series exhibited strong absorption bands at 265 nm and 312 nm. These bands corresponded to the aromatic π − π* transitions from the C=C bond at the core state and the n–π* transition of the C=O bond at the surface state in HICs [49,50], aligning with FT-IR and XPS results. Moreover, HICs-4 still possessed the strongest UV–vis absorption based on the fact that BPs content possibly helped create functional groups that were favorable for UV–vis absorption. Subsequently, the PL emission spectra of all HICs solutions, as depicted in Fig. S5b, reveal that all HICs exhibited strong PL emission at a 360 nm excitation wavelength. The inset PL intensity graph in Fig. S5b confirms the observed PL intensity. Notably, HICs-4 displayed the strongest fluorescence emission, attributed to the fluorophores of oxygen and nitrogen functional groups on the surface of HICs-4, which agrees with the highest amount of oxygen and nitrogen heteroatoms as determined by XPS results. Herein, HICs-4 showed the strongest UV–vis absorption and PL intensity; therefore, the HICs-4 will be used as a sensor as follows.

The PL spectra of HICs-4 solutions with different excitation wavelengths from 280 to 480 nm were recorded as shown in Fig. 2 (a-c). Obviously, when HICs-4 exhibited strong PL emission between 400 and 550 nm, corresponding to the blue-green region. When the excitation wavelength was increased from 280 to 360 nm, the fluorescence intensity was enhanced, achieving a maximal fluorescence at 360 nm of excitation wavelength, after which the intensity started to decrease until the excitation wavelength reached 480 nm, as seen in Fig. 2a. These were consistent with the 2D excitation-emission contour plot (Fig. 2b), presenting an excitation-dependent emission. Interestingly, according to the normalized fluorescence spectra of HICs-4 as depicted in Fig. 2c, it can be elucidated that the emission spectra gradually shifted from 412 to 536 nm with the excitation wavelengths ranging from 320 to 480 nm. This designates the excitation-dependent behaviors of HICs-4. The excitation-dependent emission can be attributed to 1) the n − π* transition caused by the oxygen and nitrogen bonded functional groups [11], 2) the presence of multiple fluorophores, such as conjugated units and surface defects [50] created via the oxidation process [22], and 3) the heterogeneity in size. Furthermore, the emission spectrum of the HICs-4 apparent at 444 nm was achieved under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm as plotted in Fig. 2d. The inset images display the HICs-4 under daylight (left) and under UV light (right), demonstrating that the yellowish-brown color solution emits strong bluish fluorescence. Besides, the QY was 10.9 % with respect to quinine sulfate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of the HICs aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the photostability of HICs-4, the solution was exposed to UV light (λ365 nm). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were detected in the interval of 0 min up to 60 min at 360 nm excitation wavelength. Fig. S6a illustrates the trend of PL intensity, demonstrating no significant decline over time until 60 min; after that it remained steady at 0.97 of the relationship of F/F0, as presented in Fig. S6b, illustrating a promising photostability of HICs-4. The emission intensities before and after UV light irradiation are represented as F0 and F, respectively. Moreover, to confirm the photostability of HICs-4, it was evaluated from UV absorption intensity for 6 h. The UV absorption retention revealed a decreasing trend, but still remained fairly high (89.4 %) as in Fig. S6c. One possible explanation for the high stability of HICs-4 is that the carboxyl groups on the surface of HICs-4 blocked the reactive sites and sterically decreased chromophore reactivity by introducing bulky groups [22]. The impact of ionic strength on the fluorescence features of the HICs-4 was thoroughly examined. As demonstrated in Fig. S6d, the HICs-4 displayed a significant almost unchanged fluorescence signal, with no aggregation observed, even with the gradual addition of NaCl up to a concentration of 1000 mM. This indicates that HICs-4 maintain stability under high extreme ionic-strength conditions. Obviously, the long-term stability of HICs-4 under various pH conditions at 4 °C for 1 month was studied. The percent stability and UV absorption retention of HICs-4 remained unchanged at pH 4 and showed comparable stability (Fig. S6(e-f)). As a result, HICs-4 was potentially appropriate for the fluorescent nanosensor. Meanwhile, the pH-dependent PL properties of HICs-4 were investigated, with an emission peak observed at 444 nm under 360 nm excitation.

As depicted in Fig. S7(a-b), the PL intensity of HICs-4 gradually increased as the pH rose to 3 under strongly acidic conditions. The acidic environment clearly influenced the PL intensity, likely due to the protonation of HICs-4 reaching saturation in such conditions [51,52]. Although the PL intensity increased from pH 1 to 3, the long-term stability tests conducted over one week revealed aggregation in solutions within this pH range (Fig. S6e). The highest PL intensity was found to remain nearly constant as the pH varied from 3 to 10, indicating high stability across a wide pH range. This stability could be partially ascribed to the presence of C=O and OH groups on the surface of HICs-4, which are more resistant to ionization than NH2 and COOH groups [53]. Afterward, the PL intensity diminished when the pH exceeded 10, likely due to interactions involving oxygen-containing functional groups, such as COOH and OH, which induced deprotonation phenomena on the surface of HICs-4 [11,54]. This interaction led to a reduction in PL intensity under strongly basic conditions. These findings suggest that HICs-4 possessed excellent and stable fluorescence properties across a broad pH range (4–10) that is similar to physiological environments (pH 7–8) [55], living cells (pH 4–8) [56], herbal drinks (pH 3–7), and dairy milk (pH 6.5–6.7) [57]. Therefore, the designed HICs-4 probe demonstrates significant potential for applications in environmental and biological systems.

The optical properties of the as-prepared HICs-4 were studied to evaluate its effectiveness as a fluorescent probe, focusing on its behavior in the presence of different metal ions and small organic molecules. As presented in Fig. 3 (a-b), the fluorescence quenching interference of different metal ions, including Bi3+, Cd2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Na+, Li+, and Cu2+ and of different small organic molecules, including L-ascorbic acid, citric acid, tartaric acid, gluconic acid, mandelic acid, D-glucose, ammonia, urea, thiourea, L-alanine, glycine, caffeine, and albutin. All analytes were tested at a concentration of 200 μM in the HICs-4 solution. Additionally, fluorescence quenching was examined by plotting the relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) on the y-axis, where F0 denotes the PL intensity of pure HICs-4 and F represents the PL intensity of HICs-4 in the presence of Fe3+. The results indicated that while most ions (Fig. 3a, blue column) and small organic molecules (Fig. 3b, green column) caused nearly no quenching effect on the PL signals, the most pronounced quenching effect was observed only in the existence of Fe3+, leading to a dramatic 55 % reduction in PL intensity compared to bare HICs-4. This demonstrates the remarkable selectivity of HICs-4 for the target analyte. Obviously, this result can be seen in the photograph (Fig. S8). It showed a nonfluorescent image of HICs-4 in the presence of Fe3+ while other samples were still emitting blue fluorescence of HICs-4 under UV illumination (λ365 nm). To further evaluate the selectivity of HICs-4 for Fe3+, the addition of Fe3+ was conducted in the HICs-4 solution containing other interfering substances. As shown in Fig. 3(a-b) (orange column), there was no significant effect on the quenched fluorescence signals, even in the presence of both interfering metal ions and small organic molecules. The results revealed that HICs-4 exhibits high selectivity for Fe3+ sensing. In addition, the selectivity study of HICs-4 can be confirmed by the fluorescence decay lifetime in Fig. 3(c-d). It was found that when HICs-4 interacted with Fe3+, it presented a lifetime of 6.0 ns, compared with 6.6 ns of the bare HICs-4. While the other interferences, such as metal ions and small organic molecules, slightly decreased the lifetimes, indicating that HICs-4 has selectivity for detecting Fe3+. Consequently, HICs-4 can serve as a fluorescence nanosensor for straightforward Fe3+ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms of fluorescence quenching in Fe3+ sensing
ce:section.ce:para:
To elucidate the feasibility of various fluorescence quenching mechanisms, which occur via the inner filter effect, static quenching, dynamic quenching, or a combination of both static and dynamic quenching [58]. Hence, we further explore the optical properties of the HICs as shown in Fig. 4 . As illustrated in Fig. 4a, the IFE can take place via two pathways. First, the overlap between the absorption band of Fe3+ ions and the fluorescence excitation of HICs-4 (blue area region) led to the competitive energy absorption between them as shown in Fig. 4b. Subsequently, the second one is the overlap between the absorption of Fe3+ ions and the fluorescence emission graph of HICs-4 (red area region), implying the transfer of emission energy. from HICs-4 to Fe3+. These two pathways led to the reduction in PL intensity of HICs-4 [59,60]. Moreover, the fluorescence emission of HICs-4 was quenched in the addition of Fe3+ ions, and the conversion from bright blue to dark blue color of HICs-4 was clearly observed in the presence of Fe3+. Not only elucidating the use of the IFE mechanism, but the standard Stern-Volmer equation is employed to investigate the potential quenching mechanism, as displayed in Eq.
(3) [61]. This describes the relationship between the quenched signals and concentrations of Fe3+, where c is the concentration of Fe3+, and ksv is the quenching constant.

Subsequently, Fig. 4c shows a linear relationship, where the value of ksv was determined to be 0.0077 μM−1. Subsequently, the collision quenching constant (kq ) was calculated as 1.15 × 1012 M−1·s−1 using Eq.
(4) [61], where τ0 is the fluorescence decay lifetime.

k q

The calculated collision quenching constant significantly exceeds the theoretical dynamic quenching value of 2.0 × 1010 M−1·s−1, suggesting the presence of a static quenching mechanism (SQM) [11,22,62], which occurs due to the formation of a ground-state non-fluorescent complex through the interaction between HICs-4 and Fe3+ [63]. Additionally, the effects of temperature can be utilized to elucidate the distinguishing forms of quenching. The observation of a reduction in quenching with rising temperature supports the presence of static quenching [64], as higher temperatures decreased the stability of the HICs-4/Fe3+ complex, leading to a recovery of fluorescence emission, as depicted in Fig. 4d. All the aforementioned observations confirm a static quenching mechanism similar to the finding from the Stern-Volmer equation. To further support the quenching process, the most distinguishing approach for identifying the existence of interactions and analyzing PL decay lifetime is shown in Fig. 5 a. The PL decay lifetime of HICs-4 in solution was 6.64 ns without Fe3+ and decreased to 6.05 ns with 200 µM Fe3+. When the Fe3+ concentration was increased to 500 µM, the lifetime further decreased to 4.31 ns. Notably, the decay curves of HICs-4 were altered along distinct pathways upon Fe3+ addition. These findings suggest the presence of a dynamic quenching mechanism [58]. While considering the relative decay lifetime (τ0/τ) of HICs-4 with and without Fe3+, this evidence can be attributed to apparent dynamic quenching, as τ0/τ deviates from 1 (where τ0/τ = 1 indicates static quenching) [65]. The UV − vis spectra of HICs-4 with varying Fe3+ concentration ns, shown in Fig. S9a, revealed that the UV absorption intensity increased with Fe3+ concentration. However, no new peak appeared in the HICs-4 absorbance, suggesting dynamic quenching caused by collisions between excited HICs-4 and Fe3+ [66]. Thus, it is reasonable to conclude that both the IFE and a combination of SQM and dynamic quenching mechanisms played a role in the observed fluorescence quenching of HICs-4 in the presence of Fe3+. Consequently, the HICs-4 developed in this study offer a valuable and highly sensitive approach for detecting Fe3+ in solution, suggesting their potential use as sensors for Fe3+ detection in various environmental, biological, and food contexts.

To explain the phenomenon of the unique interactions between Fe3+ and HICs-4, as illustrated in Fig. 5b, the study reveals that the fluorescent quenching of HICs-4, with a quenching effect (F/F0) of 0.36, is lower compared to bare CDs (0.5) and Fe3O4 NPs (0.8). This indicates that HICs-4 has a high efficiency in interacting with Fe3+. Nevertheless, the strong fluorescence quenching effects of Fe3+ on HICs-4 are due to the affinity of the hybrid nanomaterials, where iron oxide serves as the core and CDs as the surface, with their combined properties leading to enhanced interaction efficiency. As previously reported, CDs have abundant functional groups, including hydroxyl (OH), carboxyl (COOH), and amine (NH2) groups, on their surface. These functional groups contain lone pairs of electrons, resulting in a negatively charged surface that can donate electrons to Fe3+, facilitating strong coordination bonds between them [51]. In fact, it might be ascribed to the interaction of the plentiful lone pairs of electrons on the surface of HICs-4 with Fe3+, as ensured by the FTIR spectra (Fig. S10). The FTIR spectra showed significant changes in HICs-4 with the presence of Fe3+, with a notable decrease in absorbance at 1712, 1601, and 1350 cm−1, indicating the binding of Fe3+ to COOH and OH groups. This result suggests that the formation is likely of ferric-hydroxyl or ferric-carboxyl complexes on the HICs surface [51], which led to the observed PL intensity quenching. In particular, the study compares the influence of the IFE and SQM mechanisms on HICs-4, CDs, and Fe3O4 NPs in the presence of Fe3+, as shown in Fig. S11. The comparison of overlap between the absorption band of Fe3+ (analyte) and the fluorescence excitation and emission of HICs-4, CDs, and Fe3O4 NPs showed no significant differences, as depicted in Fig. S11b. This suggests that the IFE alone does not account for the varying interactions of these samples with Fe3+. The percentage reduction in decay lifetime was calculated using equation 5, where τ0 and τ0/Fe 3
+ represent the fluorescence decay lifetimes of the sample in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively.

As a result, the decay lifetime of HICs-4 shows the highest percentage reduction of 5.6 % when Fe3+ was added, followed by CDs and Fe3O4 NPs with reductions of 2.4 % and 1.0 %, respectively. This confirms that HICs-4 exhibits both collisional and complex formation interactions with Fe3+ (analyte), which are attributed to dynamic quenching and SQM, respectively, in comparison to CDs and Fe3O4 NPs (Fig. S11a). Herein, the notable quenching of HICs-4 in the presence of Fe3+ is ascribed to coordination between HICs-4 and Fe3+, likely mediated by a ligand–metal charge transfer (LMCT) mechanism [67]. This coordination leads to charge transfer within the fluorescent ligand–metal system. This hypothesis is reinforced by the paramagnetic nature of Fe3+, which has unpaired electrons in its d-orbital (3d5 configuration) [68,69]. These unpaired electrons play a crucial role in the electron transfer processes that result in fluorescence quenching. Thus, the interaction between HICs-4 and Fe3+ significantly impacts the fluorescence properties of the system, whereas comparative analysis reveals that no fluorescence quenching occurs when HICs-4 interacts with Fe2+. Although Fe2+ is paramagnetic (3d⁶ configuration), HICs-4 does not exhibit any quenching response to Fe2+. This lack of quenching can be explained through the IFE, as the absorption band of Fe2+ has a smaller peak area compared to that of Fe3+, as illustrated in Fig. S12. Consequently, there is less overlap between the absorption band of Fe2+ and the fluorescence excitation and emission of HICs-4, which then causes the absence of quenching. Therefore, a combination of CDs and Fe3O4 NPs together in HICs-4 gave a selective sensing towards Fe3+.

Additionally, the core state of HICs-4 consists of particles of Fe3O4 NP. Furthermore, the surface of iron oxide with carboxyl groups can significantly affect the electron transfer process. Because the Fe3O4 NPs structure is the octahedral site, they can easily accommodate both Fe2+ and Fe3+, producing a very interesting redox chemistry within the structure, in which the surface-bound Fe2+ sites are electron donors particularly reactive towards Fe3+ (analyte) in solution by electron transfer [70]. As mentioned above, the quenching of HICs-4 occurs due to electron transfer from the abundant electrons on the surface of HICs-4. This involves not only direct electron transfer to Fe3+ (analyte) but also the transfer of electrons to Fe3O4 NPs, which facilitates the exchange of electrons from Fe3+ to Fe2+ on the Fe3O4 NPs. Therefore, it can promote the regeneration of Fe2+, which can accelerate the interaction of HICs-4 with Fe3+ (analyte). Based on the analysis of the above results, the potential mechanisms underlying the fluorescence quenching of HICs-4, CDs, and Fe3O4 NPs in their interaction with Fe3+ were proposed and shown in Scheme 2 . This phenomenon could lead to the formation of a non-radiative electron transfer pathway and coordination interaction, resulting in a reduction in the substantial fluorescence intensity of the HICs-4.

Subsequently, the effects of various parameters on Fe3+ sensing were explored, as depicted in Fig. S13(a-d). Furthermore, the impact of different excitation wavelengths, pH conditions, and concentrations of HICs-4 on fluorescence quenching was measured.The HICs-4 displayed a high fluorescence emission at 412 nm in the absence of Fe3+. However, the highest dependence of quenching (F/F0) of HICs-4 in the presence of Fe3+ was observed at 320 nm under acidic conditions (pH 4) with a HICs-4 concentration of 0.022 mg⋅mL−1, surpassing responses recorded under other conditions. These conditions demonstrated the largest peak area overlap, as illustrated in Fig. S13(a-c), confirming the most effective IFE. This indicates that these conditions enhanced the sensitivity for Fe3+ detection.The effects of reaction time on the interactions between HICs-4 and Fe3+ ions were analyzed to measure the rapidity and stability of PL quenching efficiency, as observed in Fig. S13d. The results revealed that the reaction was rapidly completed within 5 min and then stabilized and remained steady for up to 40 min. This highlights the HICs-4 as promising candidates for fluorescence nanosensors, offering both rapid and stable detection of Fe3+.

The PL spectra of HICs-4 with different Fe3+ concentrations were obtained to assess the sensitivity of HICs-4 for the quantitative detection of Fe3+. The signals at 412 nm decreased gradually as Fe3+ concentration was increased from 0 to 500 μM, indicating that HICs-4 can serve as a turn-off sensor for iron detection (Fig. 6 a). Additionally, Fig. 6b shows a linear relationship (R2 = 0.9937) between the quenched signal and concentrations ranging from 0.06 to 100 μM, with a LOD of 13.61 nM, calculated as 3σ/slope [71,72]. This result indicates that the HICs-4 sensor possessed high sensitivity toward various concentrations of Fe3+ ions. Table S1 presents the types of fluorescence sensors, linear ranges, and possible quenching mechanisms from previous research on Fe3+ detection. The results suggest that our novel HICs-4 offers advantages in terms of a low detection limit and broad linear range, making it more effective in interacting with Fe3+ compared to other reported methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The HICs, with their high sensitivity, selectivity, and rapid detection capability for Fe3+, can be effectively applied to directly detect Fe3+ ions in real samples, such as dairy products and herbal drinks. To assess the practical utility of these HICs sensors, quantitative detection of Fe3+ was performed on samples spiked with standard solutions at concentrations of 20.0, 60.0, and 100.0 µM, all within the linear range. As shown in Table S2, the recoveries ranged from 94.8 % to 97.8 % for milk and from 90.8 % to 102.3 % for herbal drinks. These findings suggest that the as-synthesized HICs-4 can be effectively employed as a promising Fe3+ sensor, particularly in dairy and herbal drink samples.

In summary, a series of hybrid iron oxide/carbon dots (HICs) were successfully fabricated using precursors of magnetite and varying amounts of blue pea flower content. Among these, HICs-4 displayed the highest PL intensity at 444 nm upon 360 nm excitation and emitted bright blue fluorescence. Due to these characteristics, HICs-4 was selected as the optimal nanosensor for detecting Fe3+. The excitation-dependent PL behavior of HICs-4 is attributed to the n − π* transition from oxygen and nitrogen-bonded functional groups, the presence of multiple fluorophores like conjugated units, and surface defects from the oxidation process, as well as size heterogeneity. Meanwhile, the HICs-4 possessed high photostability, maintaining consistent PL intensity during UV light exposure. It also exhibited good stability under varying ionic strengths. The PL intensity of HICs-4 was prominently quenched in the presence of Fe3+ ions, exhibiting ultra-selectivity towards Fe3+ compared to other metal ions and small organic molecules. HICs-4 demonstrated a strong linear response within a concentration range from 0.06 to 100 μM, achieving a limit of detection (LOD) of 13.6 nM upon excitation at 320 nm. Beyond this, we confirm the effectiveness of HICs-4 in sensing Fe3+ by analyzing its decay lifetime, which revealed a significant percentage reduction of 5.6 %. The fluorescence-quenching mechanism associated with the interaction between HICs-4 and Fe3+ was predominantly supported by IFE, a combination of SQM and dynamic quenching, and electron transfer processes. Furthermore, the strong coordination between the Fe3+ ion and the hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amine functional groups of HICs-4 was elucidated through the LMCT mechanism. These findings elucidate the distinctive properties of HICs-4, showcasing its ultra-selective, remarkably sensitive, and rapid 'turn-off' fluorescence response to Fe3 +. Therefore, HICs-4 can be utilized as a candidate nanosensor for the precise detection of Fe3 +, a potential underscored by its successful application in testing dairy products and herbal drink samples. Ultimately, HICs-4 represents a promising nanosensor, paving the way for environmental health and biological sensing applications.
